coredata.dc:title: MOFs-mediated nanoscale Turing structure in polyamide membrane for enhanced nanofiltration
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Fan
Cao, Ming
Chen, Yingbo
coredata.dc:description: This work reported the successful preparation of a novel nano-Turing structure polyamide membrane. By introducing polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) into UiO-66-NH2, PVP-UiO-66-NH2 with high water hydrophilicity and low particle size was synthesized. The aqueous phase dispersion containing PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was loaded on the support through vacuum filtration method, and PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was fused in the polyamide layer via traditional interfacial polymerization (IP) technology. The existence of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 induced the occurrence of “local activation and lateral inhibition” in the IP system, resulting in the appearance of nano-Turing structure on the membrane surface. The successful preparation of PVP-UiO-66-NH2@polyamide composite membrane with uniform distribution of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was proved. The PVP-UiO-66-NH2@polyamide composite membrane showed excellent water permeability of 32.95 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 and stable Na2SO4 removal rate of 99.35 %, compared with 17.56 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 and 99.56 % of pure polyamide membrane under the same conditions. In addition, the PVP-UiO-66-NH2@polyamide composite membrane exhibited excellent long-term filtration stability, thermal stability and antibacterial property.

head.ce:title: MOFs-mediated nanoscale Turing structure in polyamide membrane for enhanced nanofiltration

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reported the successful preparation of a novel nano-Turing structure polyamide membrane. By introducing polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) into UiO-66-NH2, PVP-UiO-66-NH2 with high water hydrophilicity and low particle size was synthesized. The aqueous phase dispersion containing PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was loaded on the support through vacuum filtration method, and PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was fused in the polyamide layer via traditional interfacial polymerization (IP) technology. The existence of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 induced the occurrence of “local activation and lateral inhibition” in the IP system, resulting in the appearance of nano-Turing structure on the membrane surface. The successful preparation of PVP-UiO-66-NH2@polyamide composite membrane with uniform distribution of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was proved. The PVP-UiO-66-NH2@polyamide composite membrane showed excellent water permeability of 32.95 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 and stable Na2SO4 removal rate of 99.35 %, compared with 17.56 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 and 99.56 % of pure polyamide membrane under the same conditions. In addition, the PVP-UiO-66-NH2@polyamide composite membrane exhibited excellent long-term filtration stability, thermal stability and antibacterial property.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The shortage of freshwater has become a global problem [1]. As economy and population are growing rapidly, this problem will be further exacerbated in the future [2,3]. Increasing the total amount of freshwater through desalination and realizing water recycling through wastewater treatment are the key strategies to address freshwater shortage [4]. Compared with traditional separation processes (such as adsorption and distillation), membrane has been increasingly used in water treatment due to energy saving and less environmental pollution [5,6]. Due to lower operating pressure and higher water permeability compared to reverse osmosis (RO) membrane, nanofiltration is considered to be an efficient strategy for water purification [7,8].

Among many methods, interfacial polymerization (IP) has been the mainstream technology to obtain nanofiltration membrane, due to its advantages of convenience, high efficiency and excellent stability. The IP technology utilizes the condensation reaction of amine monomer and acyl chloride monomer at the water-oil interface to form a dense polyamide membrane on the surface of support membrane, which is called thin film composite (TFC) nanofiltration membrane [8–10]. Since the acyl chloride monomer is insoluble or hydrolyzed in aqueous solution, the IP process mainly occurs on the side of the organic phase, and the successive IP reaction depends on the continuous diffusion of the amine monomer. This indicates that IP is a diffusion-controlled process. Therefore, the structure of polyamide membrane can be designed to facilitate water permeability by reasonably controlling the diffusion rate of monomers in the aqueous phase [11]. Gao et al. added an organic polymer, polyvinyl alcohol to the interface, and the polyvinyl alcohol hindered the diffusion of amine monomers through hydrogen bonding, which contributed to the formation of polyamide membranes with the regular nano-stripe Turing structure arranged in a network-like. The regular nano-stripe Turing structure greatly enhanced water permeability of the membranes [12]. Tang et al. introduced cucurbituril [n] (n = 6, 8) in the aqueous phase to control the amounts of nano-chain structure on the surface of polyamide membranes for the efficient separation of isopropanol (IPA) from water. Due to the relatively large water penetration site or rough surface structure, the specific nano-scale Turing-type surface structure endowed the membrane excellent water transport property [13]. However, it has been proven that the interfacial voids caused by the nano-fillers and the polyamide matrix can create additional molecular pathways, which plays an important role in improving the water permeability of the thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane [14–17]. Jiao et al. prepared the aqueous phase by adding UiO-66-NH2 to the triethylenetetramine (TETA) aqueous solution, and the diffusion rate of TETA slowed down due to its amino groups being attracted to the Zr cluster in UiO-66-NH2. In addition, the local concentration increase of TETA induced by this attraction would initiate the initial interfacial reaction. These conditions prompted the formation of polyamide membranes with the nano-Turing surface structure to realize the effective separation of CO2/CH4 [18]. Benefiting from above excellent research works, this work proposed a novel technique to generate nano-Turing structure membranes with excellent nanofiltration performance.

In this work, highly hydrophilic PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was synthesized in one step, and it was loaded to the polyamide membrane by convenient IP technology, as shown in Scheme 1 . The aqueous phase monomer (piperazine, PIP) concentration and organic phase monomer (1,3,5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride, TMC) concentration were only 0.2 wt% and 0.15 wt%, respectively. Compared with the high concentration of aqueous phase (2.8 wt%) and organic phase (0.7 wt%) in other study [18], it was low monomers consumption and energy saving.

PVP had excellent water solubility, which improved the stability and dispersibility of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 in the aqueous phase. At the same time, the hydrophobic carbon chain of PVP inhibited the growth of PVP-UiO-66-NH2, resulting in regular and uniform small particle size MOFs [19]. All above contributed to the uniform dispersion of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 in the polyamide, and reduced the interfacial defects. The most important was that existence of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 increased the viscosity of the aqueous phase and physically hindered the diffusion of PIP. And the carbonyl group and the amino group of PIP formed hydrogen bonding, causing the local concentration of PIP to increase around PVP-UiO-66-NH2. This system confused the diffusion driven of PIP, and met the condition of “local activation and lateral inhibition”, so that the Turing structure generated [12]. Experimental results showed that the nano-Turing structure significantly enhanced the nanofiltration performance of the PVP-UiO-66-NH2@polyamide membrane. This was a new strategy to create nano-Turing structure using the combination of PVP and MOFs, as well as it realized more possibilities in the field of water treatment.

Polyethersulfone (PES) ultrafiltration membrane (MWCO = 200 kDa) was purchased from Xingda Membrane Technology Co., Ltd. (Taizhou, China). Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, MW = 10,000), zirconium chloride (ZrCl4, 98.0 %), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (2-NH2-BDC, ≥98.0 %), piperazine (PIP, 99.0 %), 1,3,5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC, 98.0 %), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, >99.0 %), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, >99.0 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5 %), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 99.0 %) and potassium chloride (KCl, 99.9 %) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polyethylene glycol (PEG, MW = 100, 200, 400, 600), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, >99.5 %), n-hexane (>99.5 %), methanol (>99.0 %) and triethylamine (TEA, >99.0 %) were purchased from Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4, ≥99.5 %) and potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4, ≥99.5 %) were purchased from Fengchuan Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Deionized (DI) and RO water was produced from ULP pure water machine (Tianjin, China).

The PVP-UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles were prepared referring to the previous work [20,21]. The reaction solution was prepared according to the molar ratio of ZrCl4:2-NH2-BDC:H2O:PVP:DMF = 50:50:50:1:25,000, and it was sonicated (400 W, room temperature) until being transparent. Subsequently, the solution was poured into a hydrothermal reactor, which was placed in a vacuum oven at 150 °C for 10 h. The obtained solid was washed (12,000 r/min) with DMF (twice) and methanol (once) for removing unreacted substances. The pellets were dried in an oven at 60 °C for 48 h and collected for later use. UiO-66-NH2 was synthesized as control following the above steps without adding PVP. All solid powders were activated in a vacuum oven at 100 °C for 2 h before use.

The polyamide composite membrane was prepared by the IP method [22]. The specific fabrication path was shown in Fig. S1. The aqueous phase was 0.2 wt% PIP aqueous solution containing 0.4 wt% TEA, and the organic phase was 0.15 wt% TMC n-hexane solution. TEA was the acid binding agent that helped neutralize the acid produced in IP process. PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was added into the aqueous phase with different concentration as the modulator, and the above dispersion was sonicated (400 W, room temperature) at least 1 h. The support membrane was soaked into RO water at least 24 h to remove the glycerin in the pores. The immersed support membrane was cut into a suitable size and fixed onto the filter element of the suction flask. And then, 30 mL aqueous phase with different concentration of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was evenly distributed on its surface by vacuum filtration. The distribution state of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 on the surface of support membrane was shown in Fig. S2. The excess aqueous phase solution was sucked away using absorbent paper from the back of the support membrane, and 5 mL organic phase was completely covered the surface for IP reaction. After 30 s, the excess organic phase was discarded, and the obtained composite membrane was placed into an oven (60 °C) to continue the reaction for 5 min. The prepared TFN membrane was stored in RO water at room temperature. According to the PVP-UiO-66-NH2 concentration of aqueous phase, the composite membranes were named TFC (0 g/L), TFN-0.01 (0.01 g/L), TFN-0.02 (0.02 g/L), and TFN-0.04 (0.04 g/L), and their PVP-UiO-66-NH2 loading mass were 0 μg/cm2, 42.44 μg/cm2, 84.88 μg/cm2, and 169.77 μg/cm2, respectively. As control, we also used the same technique to prepare composite membranes (PA-0.01, PA-0.02 and PA-0.04), in which the composite membranes were loaded with UiO-66-NH2.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement of the MOFs powders were conducted using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer (BRUKER, Germany). Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) Spectrometer was obtained by a Nicolet iS50 FTIR Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, China). The UV–Vis spectra of UiO-66-NH2 and PVP-UiO-66-NH2 in water were tested using TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Purcell, China). The concentration of UiO-66-NH2 and PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was 0.01 g/L. The dispersion was sonicated (400 W, room temperature) for at least 2 h before testing, and the UV wavelength range was 200–900 nm during testing. The Raman spectrum was obtained using XploRA PLUS laser confocal scanning imaging Raman spectrometer (Horiba, Japan). The scanning wavenumber range was 500–4000 cm−1 at room temperature, and the laser wavelength was selected at 532 nm. The pore size information of MOFs powders was obtained by a fully automatic physical and chemical adsorption analyzer (BET, Autosorb-iQ-C, Conta, USA). A transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi H7650, Japan) was used to capture the morphology and distribution of the MOFs nanoparticles. A scanning electron microscope (SEM, Gemini SEM500, Germany) was used to take pictures for the surface morphology and cross-sectional structure of the membranes, and the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDXS) of the membranes was also obtained by SEM. Its acceleration voltage was 10 kV, and the membrane sample was sprayed with a thin layer of gold (Au) and platinum (Pt). The surface roughness information of the membranes was obtained by atomic force microscope (AFM, NT-MDT Prima, Russia) with a scan area of 5 μm × 5 μm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-alpha, USA) was used to measure the element content and element state of the membranes. A thermogravimetric analyzer (TG, STA449F3, Germany) was used to evaluate thermal stability information. The membrane surface potential was measured with a Zeta potentiometer (Supass-3, Austria), and KCl solution (0.01 mol/L) was used as the background solution. The dynamic water contact angle of the membranes was measured with 1 μL water every time (Kruss DA30S, Germany). Before characterization, all membrane samples were fully dried at room temperature.

A cross-flow filtration system was used in the membrane filtration experiment, as shown in Fig. S3. The membrane filtration area was 7.07 cm2, and the test pressure was 4 bar. The feed was 1 g/L salt solution, including Na2SO4, NaCl, MgSO4, MgCl2. The salt solution was stirring continuously at 25 °C during testing. Before the test, the entire filtration device ran for 30 min at 4 bar. All test results were the average of three tests. The water flux (F) and permeability (PWP) of membranes were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively.
(1)
F = V /
A × t
(2)
PWP = V /
A × t × P
In which, V was the volume of permeate, A was the effective area of the membrane cell, t was the running time, and P was the pressure exerted on the membranes by the filtration system.

The salt rejection (R) of membranes was calculated using Eq. (3).

where, Cp was the salt concentration of permeate, and Cf was the salt concentration of feed. The salt concentration was measured by a conductivity meter [23,24]. The average membrane pore size was also tested using this device, in which the circulating solution was PEG (MW = 100, 200, 400, 600) aqueous solutions (0.2 g/L). The MWCO of membranes was corresponded to the MW of PEG at the rejection rate of 90 %. Eq. (3) was also applied to calculate the rejection rate of PEG. The concentration of PEG in feed or permeate was tested by total organic carbon (TOC) analyzer (TOC-VCPH Shimadzu, Japan). The test process was repeated three times to get the final result. The conversion Eq. (4) for MW of PEG and Stokes radius (ds) of membranes was shown below.

where, μp was the mean effective pore size, and it was the ds corresponding to the MW of PEG at 50 % rejection. σp was the geometric standard deviation, and it was the ratio of ds at 84 % rejection to 50 % rejection of PEG. When we used the Eqs. (4) and (5), we ignored the electrostatic and hydrodynamic interactions between membrane pores and neutral solutes [25].

Fig. 1 showed the morphology and water dispersion state of UiO-66-NH2 and PVP-UiO-66-NH2. Fig. 1 (a, b) was the low magnification TEM images of UiO-66-NH2 and PVP-UiO-66-NH2, and the distribution of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles in water was significantly better than that of UiO-66-NH2. Simultaneously, PVP-UiO-66-NH2 maintained good dispersion in water more than one month, while there was obvious precipitation in the UiO-66-NH2 water dispersion after two days in Fig. 1 (c). It was confirmed that the insertion of PVP greatly improved the water dispersibility of PVP-UiO-66-NH2. To observe the morphological features of nanoparticles clearly, we showed the high magnification TEM images in Fig. 1 (a′, b′). The morphology of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was no longer the regular octahedron, and its edges were more rounded than UiO-66-NH2. In conjunction with Fig. 2 , we found that the particle size of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was smaller than UiO-66-NH2, since its crystal growth was limited by the hydrophobic carbon chain in PVP [19]. Smaller particle size and better water stability enabled the excellent distribution of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 in polyamide structure [26,27].

Several methods were used to confirm the successful synthesis of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 and characterize its physical and chemical properties. In Fig. 3 (a, b), PVP-UiO-66-NH2 showed a similar FTIR spectra and XRD pattern to UiO-66-NH2, and this indicated that the addition of PVP had little effect on the internal bonding or structural arrangement of aromatic ligands and zirconium cluster units. In FTIR spectra, the peak at 765 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of ZrO [28], and the peaks of 1350–1700 cm−1 were mainly attributed to the stretching of OCO coordinated by aromatic acid and Zr4+ [29]. The CN stretching mode appeared at 1260 cm−1, and in the high frequency region, NH stretching vibrations appeared at 3350 cm−1 and 3470 cm−1 [30–32]. The above description confirmed the successful synthesis of UiO-66-NH2. Corresponding to the typical FTIR peaks of PVP, PVP-UiO-66-NH2 showed a significant enhancement of CH vibration at 658 cm−1, 1428 cm−1, 2877 cm−1, and 2947 cm−1, respectively. And the enhanced CO stretching vibration at 1650 cm−1 was also derived from PVP, confirming that PVP-UiO-66-NH2 had been successfully synthesized [33,34]. In XRD pattern, PVP-UiO-66-NH2 had the typical crystal planes ((111), (200), (600)) of UiO-66-NH2, which indicated that PVP-UiO-66-NH2 maintained the crystal structure arrangement of UiO-66-NH2 [35,36]. However, the diffraction peak intensity of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was weaker and wider than that of UiO-66-NH2, which proved that PVP reduced the crystallinity of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 and limited the growth of crystals. The UV–Vis characteristic peak of the nanoparticle in dispersant can reflect its particle size and dispersion state. In Fig. 3 (c), the UV–Vis characteristic peak of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 had a slight blue shift compared to UiO-66-NH2, which confirmed that the particle size of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was smaller than UiO-66-NH2 [37,38]. The pore size information of MOFs was obtained by adsorption-desorption isotherm and pore size distribution curve in Fig. 3 (d, e). The adsorption-desorption isotherms of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 and UiO-66-NH2 were both the curve of type I, indicating that they were microporous materials. However, when the P/P0 was >0.9, the adsorption and desorption of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 had obvious hysteresis loops. The reason was that PVP occupied some space inside the crystal, which hindered the regular arrangement of the crystal, and caused some mesoporous structure inside the crystal, as shown in Fig. 3 (e). The specific pore size information was presented in Table 1 . Due to the occupation of PVP during internal bonding of PVP-UiO-66-NH2, the specific surface area of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 dropped to 345.94 m2/g, and its average pore size also decreased compared to UiO-66-NH2. The total pore volume of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was larger than UiO-66-NH2, which was caused by some mesopores in the inner channel of PVP-UiO-66-NH2.

The amount of PVP grafted and its effect on the thermal stability of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was characterized by TG curve in Fig. 3 (f). The weight loss of MOFs was divided into three stages, which were consistent with related literatures [39,40]. The weight loss before 180 °C was mainly related to the evaporation of water and the decomposition of DMF adsorbed in the pores of the nanoparticles. At this stage, the weight loss of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was 7.13 %, and it was more than that of UiO-66-NH2. Some large mesopores of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was more restrictive to DMF and water molecule, compared with the fully microporous structure of UiO-66-NH2. The second stage was the weight loss during 180 °C–390 °C, which corresponded to the dehydration of the Zr6O4(OH)4 node to produce Zr6O6. The weight loss after 380 °C was the final stage, which was mainly due to the frame fracture and dehydration of Zr6O6 before being oxidized to ZrO2. The weight loss of PVP mainly occurred at 300 °C–470 °C. In this temperature range, the weight loss of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was 23.80 %, and the weight loss of UiO-66-NH2 was 18.31 %, so the grafted PVP accounted for approximately 5.49 % of the mass of PVP-UiO-66-NH2. The total weight loss of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was >13.44 % compared with UiO-66-NH2, which is approximately the sum of the weight loss in the first stage and the temperature range of 300 °C–470 °C. Therefore, compared with UiO-66-NH2, the excess weight loss of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 mainly came from the evaporation of solvent bound by the pores and the high temperature decomposition of PVP. It could be inferred that the access of PVP had no obvious effect on the frame structure thermal stability of PVP-UiO-66-NH2.

The expected XPS results and FTIR spectra confirmed the successful polymerization of the composite membranes and the presence of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 in the polymerization zone [41]. The XPS spectra of the membranes were shown in Fig. 4 , in which the peaks of C1s and Zr3d were separated. The expected C, N and O elements were presented in all prepared membranes, and the characteristic element Zr in PVP-UiO-66-NH2 appeared in the TFN membranes, confirming the successful synthesis of all composite membranes. Moreover, the peak intensity of Zr element became stronger with the increasing loading mass of PVP-UiO-66-NH2, which confirmed that different amounts of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 had been successfully inserted in the polyamide network. The relevant data of the XPS spectra was presented in Table 2 , which discussed the impact of the addition of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 on the polyamide layer. The polymerization degree of membrane was expressed by the content of CON group. When the PVP-UiO-66-NH2 loading mass was 42.44 μg/cm2, compared to TFC, the C/N ratio of TFN-0.01 decreased, and the CON content of TFN-0.01 increased. Therefore, a small amount of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 could promote the interfacial growth of polyamide. The reason was that the aqueous phase containing PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was loaded on the surface of support membrane by vacuum filtration, so that the aqueous phase solution was existed in not only the pores of the support membrane but also the thin PVP-UiO-66-NH2 layer. And the increased amine monomers in PVP-UiO-66-NH2 layer promoted the IP process. However, when the PVP-UiO-66-NH2 loading mass rose to 84.88 μg/cm2, compared to TFN-0.01, the CON content of TFN-0.02 dropped to 6.26 %. And its element content of C and N both dropped, while the O element content and C/N ratio rose. There were two reasons for these changes. First, there were more PVP-UiO-66-NH2 gathered at the water-oil interface compared to TFN-0.01, which blocked the contacting chance of PIP and TMC, so that the IP process retarded in the limited polymerization time. Second, due to the molecular composition of PVP-UiO-66-NH2, more O elements were brought into the polyamide layer than C element. When the PVP-UiO-66-NH2 loading mass rose to 169.77 μg/cm2, the CON content of TFN-0.04 decreased to 5.84 %, but its C/N ratio decreased compared to TFN-0.02. The reason was that the massive increase of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 brought a large amount of N elements. It was also not difficult to find that the amount of OCO declined with increasing PVP-UiO-66-NH2 loading mass, and the reason was that OCO group came from the hydrolysis of free acyl chloride in polyamide network [42]. In Fig. 5 (a), the FTIR spectra of all prepared membranes clearly showed the amide characteristic peaks of CO stretching vibration at 1610 cm−1 and the NH broad absorption band at 3400 cm−1, which was the evidence of successful preparation of polyamide layer. In the meantime, the TFN-0.04 exhibited the ZrO absorption peak at 774 cm−1, which confirmed that PVP-UiO-66-NH2 had been successfully added to the polyamide network. It needed to be explained that due to the trace addition of PVP-UiO-66-NH2, only the TFN-0.04 showed significant ZrO uptake [43].

Fig. 5 (b, c, d, e) showed the surface morphology of the composite membranes by SEM. The unique polyamide morphology depended on the diffusion of amine monomer and the initial polymerization location. In Fig. 5 (b), the surface of TFC membrane presented a typical ring-shaped ridge-valley structure, which was the trace of rapid IP reaction. The pores of the support membrane were not completely regular, and more aqueous phase solution stored in the macropores (Fig. S4). The IP reaction around the macropore was more intense because of the aggregation of amine monomers, so that some ring-shaped structure appeared like the volcano eruption [44]. The surface morphology and microstructure of TFN membranes was greatly affected by the loading mass of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles. For TFN-0.01, due to low loading mass, polyamide tended to encapsulate PVP-UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles during growing, which induced the formation of coexisting structure of nano-bulges and nano-stripes. For TFN-0.02 or TFN-0.04, the nano-bulges gradually disappeared, and the polyamide layer presented a net-like topological surface structure of nano-stripe stacking in Fig. 5 (d, e). The reasons for the change were listed below. The aqueous phase monomers were bound in both the pores of support membrane and PVP-UiO-66-NH2 layer. The long chain of PVP in PVP-UiO-66-NH2 swelled and stretched laterally in water, which increased the viscosity of the aqueous phase. The above factors all slowed down the spread rate of PIP. Moreover, the numerous carbonyl groups in the long chain of PVP interacted with the secondary amines in the PIP through hydrogen bonds, which not only reduced the diffusion rate of PIP again, but also caused the enrichment of PIP around the PVP-UiO-66-NH2 as shown in Fig. S5 [45–47]. This system satisfied the conditions forming Turing structure, that was the appropriate difference for the diffusion coefficient of the activator (PIP) and the inhibitor (TMC), leading to the phenomenon of “local activation and lateral inhibition”, as shown in Fig. 6 [12]. This diffusion-driven instability induced the local polymerization around PVP-UiO-66-NH2 to form the nano-bulge structure. With the increased loading mass of PVP-UiO-66-NH2, the rapid local polymerization gradually disappeared, and the polyamide structure tended to slowly grow along the direction of PIP enrichment, which produced the nano-stripe Turing structure. Just in Fig. 5 (d, e), the nano-stripe structure became more obvious compared to Fig. 5 (c), confirming the above conclusion. Fig. S6 verified the hydrogen bonding between PIP and PVP by Raman spectra. The CO peak of PVP at 1600 cm−1 shifted to the short-wave direction, which confirmed that the hydrogen bonding reduced the vibration frequency of CO [48,49].

The uniform surface structure of composite membranes had been confirmed by low magnification SEM images in Fig. S7 (a, b, c, d) and AFM images in Fig. S8. In addition, the EDXS images for Zr, O and N elements of TFN-0.02 confirmed the homogeneous distribution of polyamide and PVP-UiO-66-NH2 in membranes in Fig. S7 (f, g, h). These all benefited from PVP-UiO-66-NH2 with excellent water dispersibility and good compatibility with polyamide. Through the enlarged image of nano-stripe structure in Fig. S7 (e), it was observed that the nano-stripe structure buried in the top dense layer, so the initial polymerization position was the location of PIP enrichment, corresponding to the hypothesis on nano-stripe structure generation process. Fig. 7 (a, b, c, d) showed the cross-sectional images of the membranes. The membrane thickness increased with the increased loading mass of PVP-UiO-66-NH2. Based on the SEM images in Fig. 5 and the line scan results of AFM measurement in Fig. 7 (a′, b′, c′, d′), the width of nano-stripe was about 100–150 nm, and its average height was 30–40 nm. When the PVP-UiO-66-NH2 loading mass was below 169.77 μg/cm2, the size of some nano-stripe was larger than 150 nm, which was attributed to the adhesion growth caused by high local PIP concentration. When the PVP-UiO-66-NH2 loading mass rose to 169.77 μg/cm2, the PIP monomers were bound in the PVP-UiO-66-NH2 layer tightly just in Fig. S2 (c), and there was no space that possessed local high PIP concentration, so that the nano-stripe structure of TFN-0.04 was fine and uniform. This also caused the changes in membrane roughness. As shown in Table 3 , with the continuous increase in the number of nano-stripe structure, the surface roughness and actual membrane area of the TFN membranes showed an upward trend.

The physical and chemical properties of the membranes were reflected by surface water contact angle, Zeta potential and pore size information. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the increase of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 loading mass made the water contact angle of the membranes decline, due to the strong hydrophilicity of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 and the increasing surface roughness of the membranes. In Fig. 8 (b), as the loading mass of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 increased, the surface negative Zeta potential of the membranes weakened. The reason was that a large number of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 slowed down the diffusion rate of PIP, and the polymerization reaction of TFN membrane was slower than TFC membrane, so that less free acyl chloride groups converted into carboxyl groups [20]. This was consistent with the XPS results. The specific parameters of water contact angle and Zeta potential of the membranes were shown in Table 4 . We used the PEG rejection by the membranes to characterize their pore size. The addition of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 caused the MWCO of membranes to increase, just as Fig. 8 (c). The main reasons were as follows. Due to the low solubility of acyl chloride monomer in water, polymerization mainly occurred on the organic side of the interface, so that the continuous diffusion of PIP was the guarantee for the constant reaction. When the upper surface of the support membrane containing the PIP aqueous solution contacted to the TMC organic solution, the reaction started. At first, a large amount of PIP quickly reacted with TMC, forming polyamide in the pores of the support membrane. The formed polyamide blocked the diffusion of PIP, so the complete polyamide membrane was generated relying on the deeper diffusion of PIP to penetrate into the new reaction zone. When the loading mass of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 was low, the diffusion resistance of PIP was mainly the barrier of the formed polyamide. Therefore, with the continuous upward diffusion of PIP, the final generated network structure was still relatively dense. However, when the loading mass of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 increased, the diffusion of PIP was affected by the double resistance of polyamide network and PVP-UiO-66-NH2 layer. This caused the diffusion drive to be chaotic, and the polymerization rate slowed down, so that the polyamide layer became loose. In addition, the occupation of a large amount of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 leaded to a significant decrease in the density of polyamide. Therefore, the rejection of PEG by the TFN membranes decreased with the increased loading mass of PVP-UiO-66-NH2. Meanwhile, the XPS results in Table 2 also supported the same conclusion. As the loading mass of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 increased, the content of OCN decreased from 11.45 % (TFC) to 5.84 % (TFN-0.04). Fig. 8 (d) was simulated according to Fig. 8 (c), and it showed the pore size distribution of the membranes. The effective pore size of membranes increased from 0.249 nm (TFC) to 0.261 nm (TFN-0.04), and the change was consistent with the decreased membrane density with increasing PVP-UiO-66-NH2 loading mass.

The water purification ability of the prepared membranes was evaluated through the water penetration and salts rejection. In Fig. 9 (a), the rejection of Na2SO4 for all membranes was >95 %, while the maximum water permeate could reach to 35 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1. The TFN-0.02 membrane has the best performance with pure water permeate of 32.95 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 and Na2SO4 rejection of 99.35 %. Fig. 9 (b) explained the reason for the high water permeability of the membranes. The pure highly cross-linked polyamide structure blocked the membrane pores of the support membrane, so that the water penetration needed to go through the dense polyamide layer, forming an “inter-locking” effect. When PVP-UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles were incorporated into the polyamide layer, the net-like nano-stripes increased the specific surface area and roughness of the membranes, thereby increasing the water storage area of the membranes. The staggered nano-stripes created a lot of voids therein, which added the water filtration channels, and reduced the water transport resistance of the membranes. Although the water transport channel became longer with the increase of membrane thickness, the surface structure of membrane gradually loosened due to the increased loading mass of PVP-UiO-66-NH2. The increased loading mass of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 also enhanced the surface hydrophilicity of the membranes. Therefore, the water permeability of membranes increased from 17.56 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 (TFC) to 35.12 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 (TFN-0.04). The excellent Na2SO4 rejection performance of the membranes was mainly due to their small effective pore size and surface negative charge. Pore size sieving was the most important rejection principle of nanofiltration membranes. From Fig. 8 (d), the effective pore size of all membranes was <0.3 nm, which ensured the high-efficiency removal of Na2SO4. In addition, according to the Donnan effect [50], the negative charges on the membrane surface could produce strong exclusion on SO4 2−, which also leaded to the high rejection rate of Na2SO4. Therefore, the Na2SO4 rejection of TFC, TFN-0.01 and TFN-0.02 was all >99 %. The Na2SO4 rejection of TFN-0.04 was lowest among the membranes, and it was subject to its loose surface structure and the weakest negative Zeta potential compared to other membranes.

The desalination capability of the composite membrane was shown in Fig. 10 . In Fig. 10 (a), due to the concentration polarization, the water flux of TFN-0.02 for salt solution reduced compared to its pure water penetration, but it still maintained a high level (108.48–126.12 L·m−2·h−1) to compete with other nanofiltration membranes. The salt rejection of TFN-0.02 was in the order of Na2SO4 > MgSO4 > NaCl > MgCl2, and this was consistent with the desalination sequence of typical polypiperazine amide membrane. In addition, accompanying the increased PVP-UiO-66-NH2 loading mass, the rejection of the four salts by the membranes decreased in Fig. 10 (b). This change could be explained by the above-mentioned pore size sieving and Donnan effect. The rejection of MgCl2 dropped especially severely, and it dropped from 29.37 % to 23.21 %. The main reason was that, compared to other ions, Mg2+ permeated through the membrane pores more easily because of the electrostatic attraction by the negative charges on membrane surface, and the passage of Cl− was to maintain charge balance. The high rejection of Na2SO4 and MgSO4 benefited from their larger hydration radius compared with MgCl2 and NaCl, as well as the electrostatic repulsion between SO4 2− and the negative charges on the membrane surface [51,52].

Due to polyamide penetrated inside the pores of support membrane, the TFC membrane had good structural and performance stability. On the contrary, the addition of nanoparticles may lead the bonding force between the polyamide layer and the support membrane to decrease because of the generation of interfacial voids, thereby reducing the stability of the TFN membranes. To verify the efforts of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 on the stability of the membranes, we performed the stability test and the heat resistance experiment of TFN-0.02 using Na2SO4 salt solution. In Fig. 11 (a), after 180 h continuous filtration experiment, it was found that except for the slight decrease of water flux in the first 20 h, there were no significant attenuation of the flux and rejection of TFN-0.02. The long-term stability of TFN-0.02 was confirmed, which was related to the covalent attachment of the amino groups in PVP-UiO-66-NH2 and TMC. Fig. S9 confirmed the covalent reaction between PVP-UiO-66-NH2 and TMC by FTIR spectra. After reacting with TMC, the FTIR spectra of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 appeared a new CO vibration peak at 1715 cm−1, and the free NH2 vibration peak of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 in the high frequency region (3300–3500 cm−1) almost disappeared, which indicated that PVP-UiO-66-NH2 reacted with TMC to form amides [62]. Similarly, TFN-0.02 also had excellent heat resistance in Fig. 11 (b). Although the flux of TFN-0.02 continued to rise during the temperature range from 25 °C to 85 °C (from 108.48 L·m−2·h−1 to 203.72 L·m−2·h−1), its rejection of Na2SO4 only decreased by 4.38 % (from 99.35 % to 94.97 %). In addition to the bonding between the amino groups of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 and TMC, the good heat resistance was closely related to the unique structure of TFN-0.02. The nano-stripe generated along the lateral stretch of PVP successfully buried PVP-UiO-66-NH2 firmly in the polyamide layer. PVP-UiO-66-NH2 itself remained excellent stability at 85 °C, and it restricted the free movement of the inner segments of polyamide (Tg: 70–80 °C), so that no obvious interfacial defects appeared inside the membrane structure at high temperature. In Fig. S10, as a control group, we prepared the UiO-66-NH2@polyamide membranes referring to this work. The nanofiltration performance of obtained membranes was significantly worse than this work, confirming the outstanding contribution of PVP to the improvement of membrane performance. In Fig. 11 (c), compared with some recent related studies, this work had a huge advantage in water penetration, and it was expected to be mainly applied in the field of water treatment.

Antibacterial activity is an important factor affecting the anti-biofouling performance of membranes. It has been widely known that PVP is an excellent non-mildew material. Therefore, the antibacterial property of the PVP-UiO-66-NH2@polyamide composite membrane was investigated with E. coli. The specific experimental steps were shown in the support information. Fig. S11 showed the photographs of E. coli colony before and after contacting with the membranes. Referring to the control group, there were a large number of bacteria in the suspension contacted with the TFC membrane, while almost no bacterial growth in the suspension contacted with the TFN-0.02 membrane. Moreover, the bactericidal rate by TFN-0.02 membrane was over 99.9 %. These all indicated that the incorporation of PVP-UiO-66-NH2 significantly improved the antibacterial property of the PVP-UiO-66-NH2@polyamide composite membranes.

In summary, the composite membranes with nano-Turing structure were successfully prepared by incorporating PVP-UiO-66-NH2 into the aqueous phase of IP reaction. After characterization, not only the PVP improved the water dispersion stability of PVP-UiO-66-NH2, but also the hydrogen bonding between PVP and PIP increased the local concentration of PIP around PVP-UiO-66-NH2. These conditions provided the growth nucleus of Turing structure, and the composite membranes with nano-stripes were induced to synthesize. The water penetration of the membranes containing Turing structure enhanced significantly, which was up to 32.95 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, while the removal capacity for Na2SO4 reached >99 %. In addition, the membranes had excellent long-term filtration stability, thermal stability and antibacterial property.
Fan Xiao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-Original Draft, Preparation Ming Cao: Software Yingbo Chen: Writing-Review & Editing
